sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Meningococcal septicaemia is a severe bloodstream infection caused by Neisseria meningitidis and can progress rapidly to shock, purpura, organ failure, and death. Bacterial meningitis is inflammation of the meninges due to bacterial infection. Viral meningitis is often less severe, but may initially resemble bacterial disease. In the prehospital setting, early recognition, droplet precautions, symptom support, and urgent transport are key.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Fever, headache, malaise, vomiting
- Non-blanching or purpuric rash
- Tachycardia, tachypnoea, hypotension, poor perfusion, altered mental state
- Neck stiffness, photophobia, lethargy, seizures
- Shock or rapidly progressive sepsis in severe disease

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Rapid-onset sepsis with or without a non-blanching rash may indicate meningococcal disease
- Meningococcal disease is transmitted by close contact and respiratory droplets
- Because bacterial and viral meningitis may be difficult to distinguish clinically at first presentation, suspected bacterial meningitis or meningococcal disease should be treated as time critical
- Ensure appropriate PPE, especially during airway procedures
- All patients with suspected meningitis or meningococcal disease require urgent transport
- Do not allow the absence of rash to lower concern when sepsis, neck stiffness, altered conscious state, or rapid deterioration are present

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Apply droplet precautions and minimise unnecessary exposure
- Administer oxygen if hypoxaemic, shocked, or in respiratory distress
- Record vital signs, especially blood pressure, oxygen saturation, temperature, perfusion, and conscious state
- Consider early IM antibiotics only where strongly indicated by local protocol or senior medical direction
- Provide pain relief if suitable and not delaying evacuation
- Maintain a low threshold for RFDS or senior consultation and urgent transport

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Gain IV access if shock, seizure management, or IV therapy is likely to be required
- Administer intravenous fluids if indicated, with repeated reassessment for perfusion and fluid overload
- Administer IV antibiotics only in consultation with the on-call doctor and local protocol
- Treat seizures, airway compromise, or sepsis-related deterioration in line with the relevant guideline
- Apply ECG or additional monitoring if shock, altered conscious state, or an alternative diagnosis is being considered

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Ensure appropriate staff follow-up after significant exposure
- Ensure public health notification requirements are completed
